questions.stem: In the Agent Platform's envelope encryption scheme, what is the relationship between DEKs and KEKs?

options.text: DEKs are master keys stored in the KMS that directly encrypt application data
DEKs encrypt application data, and KEKs from the KMS provider wrap (encrypt) the DEKs so they can be stored safely
KEKs encrypt application data, and DEKs are backup keys used when the KMS is unavailable

questions.explanation: Envelope encryption uses a two-layer hierarchy: Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) are 256-bit AES-GCM keys that encrypt and decrypt application data. Key Encryption Keys (KEKs) are master keys managed by the KMS provider that wrap (encrypt) DEKs for safe storage. The KEK never leaves the KMS. This design means the KMS only handles a small number of wrap/unwrap operations per DEK, while the majority of cryptographic operations happen locally with AES-256-GCM.

questions.stem: Each unique combination of tenantId, projectId, and ________ gets its own independent DEK, ensuring per-scope key isolation.
questions.explanation: DEKs are scoped to three dimensions: tenantId (workspace isolation), projectId (project isolation, or '_tenant' for workspace-scoped models), and environment (separates development, staging, and production keys, or '_shared' for environment-agnostic models). This means a compromised DEK in one project's staging environment does not affect other projects or production data.

questions.stem: Your organization uses AWS KMS as the tenant default (level 4). A high-security financial project needs Azure Managed HSM for production only. How should you configure the KMS inheritance chain?

options.text: Set Azure Managed HSM at the tenant level so it applies everywhere, then override non-financial projects with AWS KMS
Set a Project + Environment override (level 1) for the financial project's production environment with Azure Managed HSM; all other scopes inherit the tenant default AWS KMS
Set Azure Managed HSM at the project default level (level 2) for the financial project, which automatically applies only to production

questions.explanation: The KMS configuration follows a 5-level inheritance chain with the most specific match winning. The correct approach is to keep AWS KMS as the tenant default (level 4) and override only the financial project's production environment at level 1 (Project + Environment). This ensures all other projects and the financial project's non-production environments still use AWS KMS, while only production in the financial project uses Azure Managed HSM.

questions.stem: A tenant needs to be permanently deleted, and all their data must be rendered unrecoverable without locating and deleting individual records across multiple databases. Which mechanism achieves this?

options.text: Running a batch deletion job that finds and removes every document belonging to the tenant
Crypto-shredding — destroying all DEKs for the tenant, which renders the ciphertext permanently unreadable
Rotating the KEK to a new key, which automatically invalidates all encrypted data

questions.explanation: Crypto-shredding permanently destroys all data encrypted by a DEK by destroying the key itself. Without the DEK, the ciphertext is unrecoverable, even though the encrypted records remain in the database. The DEK lifecycle follows NIST SP 800-57: active (encrypt + decrypt), decrypt_only (rotated), destroyed (wrapped DEK zeroed, data unrecoverable). This supports tenant deletion, GDPR right to erasure, and environment decommissioning.

questions.stem: DEK rotation is triggered automatically by two conditions. One is time-based expiry (dekEpochIntervalHours, default 24 hours). What is the other automatic trigger?

options.text: When the total size of encrypted data exceeds a configurable threshold
When the DEK's usage count exceeds dekMaxUsageCount (default approximately 1 billion operations)
When any pod in the cluster detects a cache miss for the DEK

questions.explanation: DEKs rotate automatically based on two triggers: time-based expiry controlled by dekEpochIntervalHours (default 24 hours, minimum 12 hours), and a usage ceiling controlled by dekMaxUsageCount (default 2^30, approximately 1 billion). When either trigger fires, the old DEK transitions to 'decrypt_only' status and a new DEK is generated. Existing ciphertext remains readable because each ciphertext embeds the DEK identifier that encrypted it.
